coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots-based interfacial nanofluid for high-performance solar-driven water evaporation

Nguyen, Vanthan
coredata.dc:description: Solar steam generation through volumetric heating using nanofluids is a promising approach for wastewater treatment and desalination. However, low evaporation rate and slow response time to the change in solar intensity seriously affect their cumulative evaporation performance in practice. Here, we propose an interfacial nanofluid structure for high-performance solar-driven water evaporation using carbon nanodot (CDs) nanofluid and airlaid paper. CDs nanofluid transferred down through the paper from a source water tank to form an interfacial evaporation structure and ensure continuous water supply for evaporation. The solar conversion heat was only localized on a small amount of nanofluid on the paper resulting in low heat loss to the bulk nanofluid and a fast response time of within 2 min to reach a steady evaporation rate. In addition, the flowing nanofluid on the paper can absorb environmental energy to achieve high-rate evaporation of 1.93 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. This study provides an effective strategy to improve the performance of volumetric heating systems for solar wastewater treatment and desalination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Nanofluids
Interfacial water evaporation
Wastewater treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and photothermal properties of CDs
Optical and photothermal conversion performance of CDs-based interfacial nanofluid
Solar-driven water evaporation of interfacial nanofluid
Wastewater treatment application of interfacial nanofluids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of interfacial nanofluid evaporator.

Structures, optical, and photothermal properties of CDs. (a) The TEM image and photograph of CDs powder, (b) FTIR spectrum, and (c) absorption spectrum of CDs; (d) photographs of CDs nanofluids at different concentrations under the irradiation of daylight and UV light (365 nm); (e) transmittance spectra of CDs nanofluids at different concentrations; (f) temperature-time relationship of CDs nanofluid (1 mg/mL) under irradiation from a solar simulator (one sun) and after the irradiation is turned off.

Photothermal properties of interfacial nanofluid. (a) Images show horizontal and vertical transferring progression along the pristine paper of water and CDs nanofluid (5 mg/mL); (b) reflectance spectra of pristine paper and interfacial nanofluid; (c) IR images and (d) corresponding temperatures of bulk nanofluid and interfacial nanofluid under one sun irradiation; (e) time-dependent temperature of interfacial nanofluids at different concentrations of CDs.

Evaporative performance of interfacial nanofluid under one sun irradiation. (a) Structure of interfacial nanofluid evaporator; (b) comparison of mass change and response time between interfacial nanofluid (with a paper height of 5 cm) and bulk nanofluid; (c) temperatures at the evaporative surface and the bulk nanofluid of interfacial nanofluid; (d) evaporation rate of interfacial nanofluid with different heights of inlet and outlet sides; (e) evaporation rate of interfacial nanofluid with 5 cm height in six cycles; (f) absorption spectra of CDs nanofluid at initial and after six cycle irradiations.

Wastewater treatment application of interfacial nanofluid. (a) Mass change of interfacial nanofluid (5 mg/mL CDs) at different concentrations of MB under one sun irradiation; (b) Digital photos and absorption spectra of wastewater containing MB before and after purification.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots-based interfacial nanofluid for high-performance solar-driven water evaporation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Nguyen V. C. and Nguyen T. N. H. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar steam generation through volumetric heating using nanofluids is a promising approach for wastewater treatment and desalination. However, low evaporation rate and slow response time to the change in solar intensity seriously affect their cumulative evaporation performance in practice. Here, we propose an interfacial nanofluid structure for high-performance solar-driven water evaporation using carbon nanodot (CDs) nanofluid and airlaid paper. CDs nanofluid transferred down through the paper from a source water tank to form an interfacial evaporation structure and ensure continuous water supply for evaporation. The solar conversion heat was only localized on a small amount of nanofluid on the paper resulting in low heat loss to the bulk nanofluid and a fast response time of within 2 min to reach a steady evaporation rate. In addition, the flowing nanofluid on the paper can absorb environmental energy to achieve high-rate evaporation of 1.93 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. This study provides an effective strategy to improve the performance of volumetric heating systems for solar wastewater treatment and desalination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a promising strategy for wastewater treatment and desalination with a minimized carbon footprint, solar-driven water evaporation has attracted considerable attention due to its highly efficient energy conversion [1–6]. In conventional bottom-heating-based evaporation, solar absorber materials are placed in the bottom and heating the entire bulk water, resulting in large heat losses and low water evaporation rate [3]. Recently, volumetric heating based on nanofluids has been widely applied for direct steam generation [7–15]. By using nanofluids dispersed in solution, the volumetric heating approach can enhance light absorption and reduce surface heat losses to achieve higher evaporation efficiency [7,14,16]. Although the efficiency was improved, the volumetric heating strategy still suffers from some evident issues. First, the water evaporation rate of the volumetric evaporator is still low, typically <1 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 kW m−2 (one sun) solar irradiation [10,17,18]. Most of the conventional volumetric heating systems are designed to heat the entire body of water, which is ineffective heat localization and results in low surface temperature. Second, the volumetric system exhibits a slow response time to the change in solar intensity, requiring at least 10 min to reach a stable evaporation rate after solar irradiation. This significantly reduces the system's performance under intermittent solar radiation in practice [7,14,17]. Third, the development of highly photothermal nanofluids with low cost and robust dispersion under long-term intense solar irradiation remains challenging [9,16,19–21].

To date, various photothermal nanofluids have been developed and explored for water evaporation such as plasmonic-based [8,22,23], semiconductors [24,25], and carbon-based nanofluids [10,12,14,26–29]. In comparison with the other materials, carbon-based nanofluids possess broader absorption spectra, higher photothermal conversion efficiency, and lower cost. As a new member of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (CDs) have shown great potential for solar-driven water evaporation due to their unique combination properties of highly photothermal conversion, high aqueous solubility, ultra stability, and low cost [30]. Recent studies showed that CDs nanofluid can maintain high photothermal conversion efficiency and excellent stability in high salinity seawater and under high-intensity solar irradiation [26,31–33]. However, these CDs-based nanofluid systems still utilized a whole-water heating strategy resulting in a low evaporation rate (<1.2 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation) and slow response time to the change in solar intensity [31,32].

As a potential solar-driven water evaporation approach, interfacial heating can achieve evaporation efficiency of ∼100 % by efficiently localizing heat at the liquid/air interface, thereby reducing the heat loss to the bulk water [3]. Since the conversion heat is only used to heat a small amount of water at the interface, the interfacial heating strategy has a fast response time to the intermittent intensity of solar radiation. To further boost the evaporation rate, various novel structure designs such as cylindrical structures [34,35], flowing fluid [36–38], and paper-based heat sinks [39,40], have been proposed to absorb the environmental energy to improve the performance of the interfacial evaporator. As a result, under one sun irradiation, the evaporation rate of these evaporators exceeded the theoretical upper limit of vapor output (approximately 1.47 kg m−2 h−1) [34,35,39–43]. Thus, the effective absorption of environmental energy is a promising pathway for developing a high-performance evaporator.

Inspired by these novel interfacial evaporators, here we propose an interfacial nanofluid evaporator based on CDs nanofluid for high-performance solar-driven water evaporation. The source CDs nanofluid in an upper water tank was first wicked down into the evaporative layer by airlaid paper on the inlet side and dropped down from the end of the paper on the outlet side (Fig. 1 ). Due to their high aqueous solubility, the CDs nanofluid can effectively be transferred from the upper water tank to the lower one through airlaid paper to form an interfacial evaporation structure. Under solar irradiation, the solar conversion heat was effectively localized on a small amount of flowing nanofluid on the evaporative surface, reducing the heat loss to the bulk nanofluid and ensuring a fast response time of within 2 min to the change in solar intensity. In addition, the flowing nanofluid on the paper can absorb environmental energy to boost the evaporation rate. As a result, the CDs-based interfacial nanofluid can achieve a high evaporation rate of 1.93 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. Furthermore, the CDs nanofluid exhibited excellent stability under repeated solar irradiation. This result provides a high-performance, eco-friendly, and low-cost strategy for solar wastewater treatment and desalination.

The CDs was synthesized by solvothermal method as in previous reports [38,44]. First, urea (12 g) and citric acid (6 g) were dissolved in 40 mL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Subsequently, 30 mL of the solution was heated in a Teflon autoclave (50 mL) at 160 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the obtained solution was diluted with distilled water and centrifuged at 12000 rpm. Subsequently, the solution was further purified via dialysis for 3 days. Finally, CDs powder was obtained by airflow drying at room temperature condition.

The FTIR spectrum of CDs was obtained using a Shimadzu FTIR spectrophotometer (IRAffinity-1S). TEM images of the CDs were captured with a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL). Absorption and reflectance spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–2600, Shimadzu).

The photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs under sunlight irradiation was determined using a previously proposed design [40]. Briefly, 1 mL of CDs nanofluid (1 mg/mL) was loaded into a 10 mm quartz cuvette and placed horizontally. The sample was irradiated by a solar simulator (Aulight, CEL-HXF300-T3). The solar power densities of the incident and transmitting light through the cuvette were measured using a solar power meter (Tenmars, TM-750). A thermocouple (Tenmars, TM-321 N) was used to measure the temperature of the solution.

CDs nanofluids with different concentrations of 1, 2, and 5 mg/mL were used for photothermal characterization. Interfacial nanofluid was fabricated by immersing a corner of airlaid paper sheet (20 mm × 20 mm, density of 90 gsm) into the CDs nanofluid and waiting until the nanofluid fully dyed the paper. Subsequently, the interfacial nanofluid was placed on expanded polystyrene foam and irradiated by the CEL-HXF300-T3 solar simulator. The temperature change of interfacial nanofluid during the irradiation was recorded by FLIR TG267 thermal camera and Tenmars TM-321 N thermocouple. The photothermal performance of interfacial water and bulk nanofluid was also evaluated. The interfacial water was prepared by immersing pure airlaid paper (20 mm × 20 mm) in water. For bulk nanofluid, 4 mL of CDs nanofluid was poured into a square hole (size of 20 mm × 20 mm × 10 mm) on expanded polystyrene foam. Three thermocouple temperature sensors were used to simultaneously measure the temperature at the top, middle, and bottom of the bulk nanofluid.

All the water evaporation tests were performed under room conditions, at a temperature of 25 °C and humidity of 40 % ± 5 %. A high-accuracy analytical balance (Shinko Denshi, Vibra AF-220E) was used to record the mass change of water during evaporation. The light source was an Aulight CEL-HXF300-T3 solar simulator. Temperature measurements were recorded using a thermal camera (FLIR TG267) and thermocouple temperature sensor (TM-321 N, Tenmars).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and photothermal properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: After solvothermal treatment and purification, black CDs powder was obtained (Fig. 2a). The morphology and chemical structure of CDs were characterized by TEM and FTIR spectroscopy. In the TEM image, CDs exhibited monodispersed particles with an average size of approximately 3.6 nm. The FTIR spectrum revealed abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, including OH/NH bonds (3100–3500 cm−1), C=C/C=N bonds (1630 cm−1), CH bond (1380–1450 cm−1), and CS bond (1050–1160 cm−1) (Fig. 2b). Due to the functional groups might create different surface states with low-energy level beside the intrinsic energy level of carbon core [45–48], the absorption spectrum of CDs can be extended to the long-wavelength region. In addition, these abundant surface functional groups are also crucial to the water solubility of CDs.

The CDs showed strong absorption in a wide range of wavelengths, from UV to IR region (Fig. 2c). As the concentration of CDs solution increased, the absorption was significantly enhanced. The CDs solution gradually turned to black color when the concentration increased from 1 μg/mL to 5 mg/L (Fig. 2d). At a very low concentration of 1 μg/mL, CDs solution exhibited high transmittance (> 90 %) (Fig. 2e). The transmittance in the long-wavelength regions dropped with the increase of CDs concentration. At the concentration of 1 mg/mL, the transmittance is approximately zero across the entire UV–vis-NIR spectrum, indicating CDs has great potential as a solar absorber for conversing solar energy. The enhancement of absorption at high concentrations of CDs solution can be attributed to the electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding between CDs [49,50].

Although possessing intense light absorption capacity, CDs solution exhibited weak photoluminescence emission, especially at high concentrations (Fig. 2d), indicating highly efficient photothermal conversion. To determine the photothermal conversion efficiency, the CDs nanofluid with concentration of 1 mg/mL was irradiated with the solar simulator, and their temperature was recorded using an experimental setup similar to our previous report [40]. Under solar irradiation (1.14 sun), the temperature of CDs solution increased dramatically from an initial temperature of 25.8 °C to 35.7 °C after 900 s irradiation (Fig. 2f). The temperature of the solution decreased quickly to room temperature after turning off the solar simulator indicating a high heat dissipation rate to the external environment. The internal photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs solution was 88.4 % (see Supplementary material for details), indicating that the CDs efficiently converted most of the absorbed light into heat. The outstanding photothermal properties of CDs hold promise for solar–thermal conversion applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photothermal conversion performance of CDs-based interfacial nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-based interfacial nanofluid was prepared by transferring CDs nanofluid through airlaid paper sheet. Transferring capacity of CDs nanofluid through the paper sheet is essential to form the interfacial nanofluid structure. As shown in Fig. 3a, high-concentration CDs nanofluid (5 mg/mL) can effectively transfer through paper sheet (size of 30 mm × 15 mm). In the horizontal direction, the transfer distance of CDs nanofluid was about 6 cm after 60 s, which was nearly the same as the transfer distance of pure water. The transfer distance of CDs nanofluid can reach 8 cm after 150 s. In the vertical direction, the transfer speed and distance of the CDs nanofluid were higher than in the horizontal direction due to the effect of gravity. The CDs nanofluid can transfer down through the paper sheet a distance of 14 cm after 150 s. The water and CDs nanofluid transferred through the paper in the vertical direction were collected and weighted to determine the flow rate. The flow rate of pure water and CDs nanofluid were 0.59 and 0.43 g min−1, respectively (Fig. S2). The flow rate of CDs nanofluid at high concentration maintained high in comparison with that of pure water, indicating that CDs can effectively transfer through the paper due to their excellent water solubility.

The CDs interfacial nanofluid possessed good light absorption capacity in a wide range across from the visible to NIR region. The reflectance of CDs interfacial nanofluid was <2 % in the region 200 to 1000 nm and still <8 % in the 1000–1400 nm range (Fig. 3b). In contrast, the reflectance of pristine paper in the visible and NIR regions was >35 %, indicating that the good light absorption capacity of interfacial nanofluid originated from the intense light absorption of CDs nanofluid.

CDs-based interfacial nanofluid was irradiated with solar light, and its photothermal performance was compared with that of bulk nanofluid and conventional CDs-coated paper. The time-dependent temperatures of interfacial and bulk nanofluids under one sun irradiation are shown in Fig. 3c and d. The temperature of the interfacial nanofluid increased quickly from an initial temperature of 22.5 °C to a stable temperature of 34.8 °C after 120 s irradiation. In contrast, the temperature of bulk nanofluid slowly increased and its top surface temperature was only 23.9 °C after 120 s irradiation (Fig. S3). As the CDs concentration increased, the stable temperature of the interfacial nanofluid also increased, as shown in Fig. 3e. In comparison with conventional CDs-coated paper, although both structures can reach nearly the same stable temperature after 120 s irradiation, the interfacial nanofluid exhibited a faster increase rate at the initial stage of the irradiation (Fig. S4). This can be explained by the effective light absorption and direct heat transfer from CDs to the surrounding water in the interfacial nanofluid structure. The results indicate that CDs-based interfacial nanofluid can effectively absorb solar light and convert it to heat for water evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-driven water evaporation of interfacial nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: We designed a flowing nanofluid evaporator for evaporation testing, as shown in Fig. 4a and Fig. S5. The source CDs nanofluid in a water tank was first wicked down into the evaporative layer by airlaid paper on the inlet side and dropped down from the end of the paper on the outlet side to a lower empty water tank. The airlaid papers on the inlet and outlet sides were at the same height. The evaporative layer was horizontally placed and irradiated by a solar simulator. The interfacial nanofluid exhibited fast response time and higher evaporation rate in comparison with conventional bulk nanofluid (Fig. 4b). Under one sun irradiation, the interfacial nanofluid (paper height of 5 cm) can reach a steady evaporation rate of 1.93 kg m−2 h−1 within 2 min. In contrast, the evaporation rate of conventional bulk nanofluid was only 0.73 kg m−2 h−1 after nearly 12 min irradiation. Temperature measurements of the evaporative surface showed a rapid increase from 23.4 °C to a steady temperature of 32.0 °C after irradiation (Fig. 4c). The temperature of bulk nanofluid in the water tank remained unchanged during the irradiation. The IR images of the evaporator before and after reaching the stable state under sunlight irradiation showed that the temperatures of both inlet and outlet papers were nearly unchanged and maintained lower than the environmental temperature (25.0 °C) (Fig. S6). These results indicate that the solar conversion heat was effectively localized on the evaporative surface, reducing the downward conduction loss to the bulk nanofluid. Furthermore, the flow nanofluid may take heat from the evaporative surface and transfer it to the lower water tank. Based on the temperature difference at the outlet before and after solar irradiation, the flow heat loss was very low (3.9 %) (Fig. S7).

It is worth noting that, in addition to effective heat localization, environmental energy also contributed to enhancing evaporation. The evaporation rate of the interfacial nanofluid under one sun irradiation significantly increased with the increase of the height of papers on the inlet and outlet sides (Fig. 4d). Because the temperatures of both inlet and outlet were constantly lower than the environmental temperature, the papers on the inlet and outlet sides can absorb environmental energy to enhance the overall evaporation rate [34,38,39]. As a result, the evaporation rate of the interfacial nanofluid evaporator was much higher than that of conventional bulk nanofluid systems [32] and comparable with previous highly efficient interfacial evaporators [36,51]. In addition, the response time to the change in solar intensity was significantly improved (within 2 min to reach the stable state), which is crucial for the performance of the evaporator under intermittent solar irradiation. We further evaluated the stability of the interfacial nanofluid evaporator. The evaporation rate of the interfacial nanofluid stayed stable after six cycles of evaporation experiment (Fig. 4e). The absorption spectrum of CDs was nearly unchanged after the repeated irradiation (Fig. 4f), suggesting the excellent stability of the CDs-based interfacial nanofluid for long-term application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wastewater treatment application of interfacial nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high performance and good stability, the CDs-based interfacial nanofluid can be used for evaporative wastewater treatment and solar desalination systems. For evaporative wastewater treatment, the evaporator can be effective for concentrating waste solution with solar energy before the additional treatment stage [35]. In practice, the separation and recycling of nanofluids as well as hazardous materials from wastewater is critical and still a challenge that needs to be addressed. Recent studies show that CDs can be modified by Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles to form a recyclable nanofluid so that the nanofluid can be separated quickly using an external magnetic field [52–54].

As an example application in wastewater treatment, the interfacial nanofluid evaporator with a paper height of 5 cm was used to treat wastewater contaminated by methylene blue (MB). MB was added into the CDs nanofluid (CDs concentration of 5 mg/mL) to form different MB concentrations. The evaporation rate of the interfacial nanofluid was changed after adding MB dye. The evaporation rate significantly increased with the increase of MB concentration and reached 2.85 kg m−2 h−1 at MB concentration of 5 μg/mL (Fig. 5a and Fig. S8). Further increasing the MB concentration, however, the evaporation rate gradually decreased. The comparison of absorption spectra showed no evident change in the absorption of CDs nanofluid after adding MB dye (Fig. S9). In addition, the dark evaporation rates of the CDs interfacial nanofluid with and without MB dye were the same (Fig. S10). Therefore, MB dye might interact with CDs to enhance the light-to-heat conversion of CDs, subsequently increasing the evaporation rate. To evaluate the content of MB in the vapor, the interfacial nanofluid evaporator was placed in a simple condensation device and was placed outdoors for evaporation and collection of clean water (Fig. S11). After the purification, the MB dye in the condensate water was significantly reduced. For practical evaporation of wastewater, the interfacial nanofluid evaporator can be placed outdoors without condensation part, and clean vapor is diffused directly into the atmospheric environment. In this case, a higher rate of water evaporation can be achieved due to the effects of wind [35].

In summary, we proposed an interfacial nanofluid structure based on CDs nanofluid and airlaid paper for high-performance solar-driven water evaporation. Highly photothermal conversion CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method. Due to their high aqueous solubility, CDs nanofluid can effectively transfer down through the paper to form an interfacial evaporation structure. The interfacial nanofluid exhibited excellent thermal localization and fast response time for the change in solar intensity. Furthermore, the flowing nanofluid on the paper can effectively absorb energy from the environment to boost evaporation. Under one sun irradiation, the evaporation rates of the interfacial nanofluid were 1.93 and 2.85 kg m−2 h−1 for pure water and wastewater. The interfacial nanofluid has great promise toward a high-performance and eco-friendly strategy for solar-driven wastewater treatment and seawater desalination.

Nguyen Van Canh: Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Nguyen Thi Nhat Hang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Nguyen Trong Cuong: Investigation. Cu Thi Anh Tuyet: Visualization, Investigation. Nguyen Ngoc Ha: Resources. Le Thi Hong Phong: Resources. Phuoc Huu Le: Writing – review & editing. Tran Le Luu: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Van-Duong Dao: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Vanthan Nguyen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by Van Lang University, Vietnam under grant number VLU-RP-232449. The authors acknowledge resources and support from Ngo Quyen University and Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy, Vietnam.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111551.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar-driven photothermal nanostructured materials designs and prerequisites for evaporation and catalysis applications

F. Zhao, Y. Guo, X. Zhou, W. Shi, G. Yu, Materials for solar-powered water evaporation, Nat. Rev. Mater. 5 (2020) 388-401.
P. Tao, G. Ni, C. Song, W. Shang, J. Wu, J. Zhu, G. Chen, T. Deng, Solar-driven interfacial evaporation, Nat. Energy 3 (2018) 1031-1041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar absorber material and system designs for photothermal water vaporization towards clean water and energy production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Gao, L. Zhu, C.K. Peh, G.W. Ho, Solar absorber material and system designs for photothermal water vaporization towards clean water and energy production, Energy Environ. Sci. 12 (2019) 841-864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based sunlight absorbers in solar-driven steam generation devices

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances and challenges for solar-driven water evaporation system toward applications
